rows.desc: A collection of elements stored at contiguous memory locations, accessed by index
Iterating through each element of an array from start to end
Adding an element at a specific index, shifting subsequent elements
Removing an element at a specific index, shifting subsequent elements
Scanning each element one by one to find a target value
Dividing a sorted array in half repeatedly to locate a target value
An array of arrays forming a grid/matrix structure accessed by row and column indices
A resizable array that doubles capacity when full (e.g., ArrayList, Python list)
Precomputed cumulative sums to answer range sum queries in constant time
Technique to find subarrays/substrings satisfying a condition using a moving window
Using two indices moving toward each other or in the same direction to solve array problems
Finds the maximum sum contiguous subarray using dynamic programming
A sequence of characters stored as an array; immutable in many languages
Reversing the order of characters in a string
Verifying if a string reads the same forwards and backwards
Determining if two strings contain the same characters with the same frequencies
Converting a string to a numeric hash for fast equality checks and pattern matching
Knuth-Morris-Pratt pattern searching using a failure function to avoid redundant comparisons
Rolling hash-based substring search that checks multiple patterns efficiently
Computes the Z-array to find all occurrences of a pattern in a string in linear time
Finding the longest sequence present in both strings in the same order (not necessarily contiguous)
Finding the longest contiguous sequence of characters common to two strings
Minimum number of insert, delete, replace operations to convert one string to another
Tree structure for efficient string storage and prefix-based search
Linear data structure where each node points to the next node; no backward traversal
Each node has pointers to both next and previous nodes, enabling bidirectional traversal
Last node points back to the first node, forming a circle
Inserting a new node at the beginning of the linked list
Inserting a new node at the end of the linked list
Removing a node by updating the previous node's next pointer to skip the target
Reversing the direction of all next pointers in a linked list iteratively or recursively
Using slow and fast pointers to detect if a linked list contains a cycle
Combining two sorted linked lists into a single sorted linked list
Using slow/fast pointer technique to find the middle element of a linked list
Finding the nth node from the tail using two pointers separated by n steps
LIFO (Last In First Out) data structure supporting push and pop operations
FIFO (First In First Out) data structure supporting enqueue and dequeue operations
Allows insertion and deletion from both front and back ends
Queue where each element has a priority; highest priority element is dequeued first
Stack maintaining elements in increasing or decreasing order for next/previous greater problems
Simulating LIFO behavior using one or two FIFO queues
Simulating FIFO behavior using two stacks with lazy transfer
Stack that supports retrieving the minimum element in constant time using auxiliary stack
Fixed-size queue using an array in circular fashion to avoid wasted space
Using a stack to verify that all brackets open and close in correct order
Tree where each node has at most two children (left and right)
Binary tree where left subtree has smaller values and right subtree has larger values
Visiting left subtree, then root, then right subtree; gives sorted output for BST
Visiting root, then left subtree, then right subtree; used to copy trees
Visiting left subtree, then right subtree, then root; used to delete trees
Breadth-first traversal of a tree level by level using a queue
Maximum depth from root to any leaf node, computed recursively
Finding the deepest node that is an ancestor of two given nodes in a tree
Self-balancing BST where height difference between left and right subtrees is at most 1
Self-balancing BST with color properties ensuring O(log n) height
Tree for storing intervals, enabling range queries and point updates efficiently
Binary Indexed Tree for prefix sum queries and point updates using binary representation
Tree where each node can have at most N children
Prefix tree for efficient string storage and retrieval, each edge represents a character
Longest path between any two nodes in a tree, may or may not pass through root
Complete binary tree where parent node is always smaller than or equal to its children
Complete binary tree where parent node is always greater than or equal to its children
Converting an arbitrary array into a valid heap structure in linear time
Adding an element to a heap and restoring heap property by bubbling up
Removing the root element and restoring heap property by sifting down
Sorting algorithm using a max heap to extract elements in descending order
Finding top K largest elements using a min heap of size K
Using a min heap to merge K sorted lists efficiently by extracting minimums
Maintaining two heaps (max-heap and min-heap) to find median of a data stream
Data structure mapping keys to values using a hash function for O(1) average lookup
Function converting a key to an array index; good functions minimize collisions
Handling hash collisions by storing multiple items at the same index using linked lists
Resolving collisions by probing for the next available slot (linear, quadratic, double hashing)
Ratio of elements to table size; high load factor increases collision likelihood
Expanding the hash table and re-inserting all elements when load factor exceeds threshold
Key-value store using hashing; provides O(1) average insert, delete, and lookup
Collection of unique elements using hashing; supports O(1) membership checks
Finding two numbers that sum to a target using a hash map to store complements
Using a hash map to count occurrences of each element in a collection
Non-linear data structure with vertices (nodes) and edges (connections) between them
Graph where edges have a direction, going from one vertex to another
Graph where edges have no direction; connection is bidirectional
Graph where each edge has an associated numerical weight or cost
2D matrix representation of a graph where cell [i][j] indicates edge between i and j
Array of lists where each index stores the neighbors of that vertex; space efficient
Explores graph level by level using a queue; finds shortest path in unweighted graphs
Explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking using a stack or recursion
Linear ordering of vertices in a DAG such that for every edge u→v, u comes before v
Finds shortest path from a source to all vertices in a graph with non-negative weights
Finds shortest paths even with negative edge weights; detects negative cycles
All-pairs shortest path algorithm using dynamic programming on a distance matrix
Builds Minimum Spanning Tree by greedily adding smallest edges that don't form a cycle
Builds Minimum Spanning Tree by greedily expanding from a starting vertex
Disjoint Set Union structure to efficiently find if two elements are in the same set
Detecting if a graph contains a cycle using DFS coloring or Union-Find
Finding all groups of vertices where every vertex can reach every other in the group
Verifying if a graph can be 2-colored such that no two adjacent vertices share a color
Repeatedly swaps adjacent elements if in wrong order; simple but inefficient for large inputs
Finds minimum element and places it at beginning; performs n-1 swaps total
Builds sorted array one element at a time; efficient for small or nearly sorted arrays
Divide-and-conquer algorithm splitting array in half, sorting, then merging; stable sort
Picks a pivot, partitions array around it, recursively sorts subarrays; in-place
Uses a max heap to extract elements in sorted order; in-place but not stable
Non-comparison sort counting occurrences of each value; efficient for small integer ranges
Sorts integers digit by digit from least to most significant using counting sort each pass
Distributes elements into buckets, sorts each bucket, then concatenates results
Generalized insertion sort with decreasing gap sizes; faster than insertion sort
Hybrid merge+insertion sort used in Python and Java; efficient on real-world data
Checks each element one by one until target is found; works on unsorted arrays
Finds target in sorted array by repeatedly halving the search interval
Applying binary search to a monotonic answer space to find optimal value
Finds max/min of unimodal function by dividing search space into three parts
Finds range where element may exist by doubling index, then applies binary search
Improved binary search for uniformly distributed data using interpolation formula
Jumps ahead by fixed blocks then performs linear search within the identified block
Technique to solve problems by breaking into overlapping subproblems and storing results
Caching recursive call results to avoid recomputation of overlapping subproblems
Filling a DP table iteratively from smallest subproblems to the main problem
Maximize value of items selected where each item can be taken at most once
Knapsack variant where each item can be taken multiple times
Finding the longest subsequence where each element is greater than the previous
Finding minimum number of coins needed to make a given amount
Finding optimal parenthesization to minimize scalar multiplications
Maximizing profit by cutting a rod into pieces and selling each piece
Determining if a subset of given set sums to a target value
Checking if an array can be split into two subsets with equal sums
Dynamic programming applied to tree structures, computing values for subtrees bottom-up
Computing values for each cell based on neighboring cells; e.g., unique paths, minimum path sum
Using bitmasks to represent subsets of elements in exponential DP problems like TSP
Makes the locally optimal choice at each step hoping to find the global optimum
Selecting maximum number of non-overlapping activities sorted by end time
Maximizing value by taking fractions of items sorted by value/weight ratio
Building optimal prefix-free binary code using a greedy min-heap based approach
Scheduling jobs with deadlines to maximize total profit
Finding maximum number of non-conflicting intervals from a given set
Finding minimum number of platforms needed so no train waits on a platform
Finding starting position to complete circular tour using greedy difference tracking
Algorithmic technique exploring all options recursively, abandoning paths that fail constraints
Placing N queens on NxN board so no two queens attack each other
Filling a 9x9 grid obeying row, column, and 3x3 box constraints using backtracking
Finding all paths from source to destination in a grid with obstacles
Generating all possible orderings of a given set of elements
Generating all possible subsets (power set) of a given set of elements
Finding all combinations of numbers that sum to a target value
Searching for a word in a 2D character grid using DFS and backtracking
Solving problems by dividing into smaller subproblems, solving, and combining results
Sorts by splitting array in half, recursively sorting halves, then merging them
Sorts by partitioning around a pivot and recursively sorting subarrays
Divides sorted array into halves to eliminate half the search space each step
Multiplies two matrices using 7 recursive multiplications instead of 8
Finds two closest points in a plane using divide and conquer with O(n log n) time
Fast multiplication algorithm splitting large numbers into smaller parts recursively
Finds maximum subarray sum by considering subarrays crossing the midpoint
Bitwise AND; result bit is 1 only if both corresponding bits are 1
Bitwise OR; result bit is 1 if at least one corresponding bit is 1
Bitwise XOR; result bit is 1 if exactly one corresponding bit is 1; used to find unique elements
Bitwise NOT; flips all bits of the operand (one's complement)
Shifts bits left by n positions; equivalent to multiplying by 2ⁿ
Shifts bits right by n positions; equivalent to dividing by 2ⁿ
Checking if the kth bit is set using (n >> k) & 1
Setting the kth bit to 1 using n | (1 << k)
Setting the kth bit to 0 using n & ~(1 << k)
Flipping the kth bit using n ^ (1 << k)
Counting number of 1-bits using Brian Kernighan's algorithm: n & (n-1) removes lowest set bit
Checking if n is a power of 2 using n > 0 && (n & (n-1)) == 0
Using integers as bitmasks to represent and iterate over all subsets of a set
Greatest Common Divisor using Euclidean algorithm: gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a % b)
Least Common Multiple computed as (a * b) / gcd(a, b)
Finds all primes up to N by iteratively marking multiples of each prime as composite
Decomposing a number into its prime factors by trial division up to square root
Performing arithmetic operations with a modulus; essential for large number computations
Computing aⁿ mod m efficiently by squaring and halving the exponent
Finding x such that a*x ≡ 1 (mod m) using Fermat's little theorem or extended GCD
Computing nth Fibonacci number in O(log n) using 2x2 matrix exponentiation
Sequence counting valid parentheses, BSTs, triangulations; Cₙ = C(2n,n)/(n+1)
Finding x satisfying multiple modular congruences simultaneously
Describes upper bound of time/space complexity as input size grows to infinity
Describes lower bound of time complexity; best case performance guarantee
Describes tight bound when upper and lower bounds are the same asymptotically
Execution time does not depend on input size; e.g., array access, hash map lookup
Time grows logarithmically; each step halves the problem size; e.g., binary search
Time grows proportionally to input size; e.g., linear search, array traversal
Common in efficient sorting algorithms like merge sort and heap sort
Time grows with square of input; e.g., bubble sort, nested loops over input
Time doubles with each additional input element; e.g., recursive fibonacci, subset generation
Extremely slow; e.g., generating all permutations of n elements
Amount of memory an algorithm uses relative to input size, including auxiliary space
Average cost per operation over a sequence of operations; e.g., dynamic array resizing
Formula to determine time complexity of divide and conquer recurrences T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)
